phase1.explanation:
Comparable and Comparator define sorting logic:

Comparable (natural ordering):
• Interface: Comparable<T>
• Method: compareTo(T other)
• Defines default ordering for the class
• Used by: Collections.sort(list), TreeSet

Comparator (custom ordering):
• Interface: Comparator<T>
• Method: compare(T o1, T o2)
• Multiple comparators for different criteria
• Used by: Collections.sort(list, comparator)

CompareTo contract:
• Returns negative if this < other
• Returns 0 if this == other
• Returns positive if this > other

Comparator returns:
• Negative if o1 < o2
• 0 if o1 == o2
• Positive if o1 > o2

Common patterns:
• Integer.compare(a, b)
• Double.compare(a, b)
• String.compareTo(other)

Lambda comparators (Java 8+):
• (s1, s2) -> s1.name.compareTo(s2.name)

Utility methods:
• Comparator.comparing(Class::method)
• Comparator.comparing(...).reversed()
• Comparator.comparing(...).thenComparing(...)

When to use:
• Comparable: for a single, natural ordering
• Comparator: for multiple or custom orderings

        Collections.sort(people);  // ERROR!

        upper bounds: Comparable<? super T>)

phase3.prompt:
Create a class Product with fields: id (int), name (String), price (double).

1. Implement Comparable<Product> by id (natural order)
2. Create a NameComparator that sorts by name
3. Create a PriceComparator that sorts by price (low to high)

In main:
1. Create a list of 4 products
2. Sort by ID (natural order)
3. Sort by name using NameComparator
4. Sort by price using PriceComparator

Print the list after each sort.
dialogueHints: Product implements Comparable<Product> with compareTo based on id
phase3.openingDialogue: Comparable vs Comparator — two ways to sort! 🔢
